title: End of module: Sampling and Data

questions.text: A researcher lists every employee at a company, numbers them, and uses a random number generator to select 40 of them for a survey. Which sampling method is this?
choices: Simple random sampling
Stratified sampling
Quota sampling
Opportunity sampling
questions.explanation: Every member of the population (the full numbered list, i.e. the sampling frame) has an equal chance of selection via a random number generator — this is the definition of **simple random sampling**.

questions.text: A school has 320 Year 12 students and 280 Year 13 students. A stratified sample of 60 students is taken, proportional to year group size. How many Year 13 students are sampled?
A market researcher stands outside a supermarket on a weekday morning and interviews whoever walks past until she has 50 responses. Which sampling method is this, and what is its main weakness?
choices: Systematic sampling; it needs a complete sampling frame
Opportunity sampling; it is likely to be biased, since only people free to shop on a weekday morning can be selected
Stratified sampling; it under-represents small strata
Simple random sampling; every shopper has an equal chance of selection
questions.explanation: Interviewing whoever happens to be available is **opportunity (convenience) sampling**. Its key weakness is that the sample is not representative — for example, people working standard office hours will rarely be free to shop on a weekday morning, so their views are excluded.

questions.text: Which of the following are genuine weaknesses of systematic sampling (selecting every $k$-th member of a list)?
choices: It cannot be used unless a sampling frame (ordered list) exists
It always requires knowledge of population strata
A hidden periodic pattern in the list matching the sampling interval can cause severe bias
It gives every member of the population an equal chance of selection before the random starting point is chosen
questions.explanation: Systematic sampling needs an ordered sampling frame (so choice 1 is a real limitation), and a periodic pattern aligned with the interval $k$ can badly bias the sample (choice 3). It does not require strata (that's stratified sampling), and the equal-chance property before the random start is a strength, not a weakness.

questions.text: Which of these variables is an example of continuous quantitative data?
choices: The number of pets owned by a family
The colour of a car
The mass, in kg, of a new-born baby
The shoe size of a customer
questions.explanation: Mass is measured, not counted, and can take any value within a range (e.g. 3.482 kg), so it is continuous. Number of pets and shoe size are discrete (countable, fixed possible values); car colour is qualitative.

questions.text: Which of the following are examples of discrete data?
choices: The number of goals scored in a football match
The height of a sunflower in cm
The number of pages in a book
The time taken to run 100 m, in seconds
questions.explanation: Goals scored and number of pages are both whole-number counts with no possible values in between (discrete). Height and time are measured and can take any value in a range, so they are continuous.

questions.text: A data set (already sorted) is $12, 15, 15, 18, 21, 22, 22, 22, 25, 29, 34$. Find the mean, correct to 1 decimal place.
For the same data set $12, 15, 15, 18, 21, 22, 22, 22, 25, 29, 34$, find the median.
questions.explanation: With $n = 11$ values already sorted, the median is the $\frac{11+1}{2} = 6$th value, which is $22$.

questions.text: For the same data set $12, 15, 15, 18, 21, 22, 22, 22, 25, 29, 34$, find the interquartile range (IQR).
questions.explanation: Splitting either side of the median (position 6) gives a lower half $12,15,15,18,21$ with median $Q_1 = 15$, and an upper half $22,22,25,29,34$ with median $Q_3 = 25$. So $\mathrm{IQR} = 25 - 15 = 10$.

questions.text: Ten students recorded the number of texts they sent yesterday: $12, 14, 15, 15, 16, 18, 19, 20, 21, 47$. Using the $1.5 \times \mathrm{IQR}$ rule, what is the upper fence for outliers?
questions.explanation: Lower half $12,14,15,15,16$ gives $Q_1 = 15$; upper half $18,19,20,21,47$ gives $Q_3 = 20$. $\mathrm{IQR} = 5$, so the upper fence is $Q_3 + 1.5 \times \mathrm{IQR} = 20 + 7.5 = 27.5$. (Since $47 > 27.5$, it is an outlier.)

questions.text: 20 students were asked how many siblings they have: 4 have 0, 8 have 1, 5 have 2, 2 have 3, and 1 has 4. Find the mean number of siblings.
For the same sibling-count frequency table (0:4, 1:8, 2:5, 3:2, 4:1, so $n=20$ and mean $=1.4$), find the population standard deviation, correct to 2 decimal places.
A box plot shows a much longer whisker on the lower side than the upper side, and the median sits closer to $Q_3$ than to $Q_1$ within the box. What term describes this shape of distribution?
questions.explanation: A long lower tail (whisker) with the median pulled towards the upper quartile indicates **negative skew**.
